title: Carved beech fruit bowl
subtitle: A gouge-hollowed fruit bowl in seasoned beech
excerpt: A round fruit bowl in seasoned beech: 25 cm diameter, 7 cm tall, hollowed with a No. 7 sweep gouge and mallet and finished with walnut oil. Beech is the ideal material for a carved bowl; uniform, hard, pale, and food-safe with an oil finish. The gouge-roughing method used here removes material faster than a hook knife alone and produces a clean floor.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for treen bowl proportions; Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry for gouge-roughing bowl interior method.

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-dried or kiln-dried timber stabilised at 8 to 12 percent moisture content. Essential for bowl carving; green wood bowls crack as they dry.
The curvature of a gouge cutting edge, numbered from 1 (flat) to 11 (deep U). A No. 7 sweep in 20 mm width is the standard bowl-roughing gouge.
A vertical cut into the wood that defines a boundary and prevents the grain from tearing past it; used to set the bowl rim before hollowing.

content.text: Beech is the traditional British bowl wood: pale, close-grained, extremely hard, and food-safe with an oil finish. A beech fruit bowl requires a gouge and mallet for the hollowing; the material is too dense for efficient hook-knife work alone. The method here uses a No. 7 gouge roughing pass to remove the bulk of the interior, then a fine gouge to smooth the floor and walls before sanding. The result is a bowl that improves with use as the walnut oil finish deepens with contact.
The wood
A block of kiln-dried beech: 27 cm diameter (or square cut to 27 cm), 9 cm thick. The grain should run along the base; from one face to the other; not across the rim.
Seasoned wood
only; green or partially dried beech will crack across the base as it finishes drying. Sycamore is an alternative: equally pale and food-safe, slightly softer and easier to hollow.
Tools
A No. 7 sweep
gouge

in 20 mm width for roughing the interior. A No. 5 sweep in 12 mm for the bowl floor and wall refinement. A carpenter's mallet to drive the gouge. A sloyd knife for the exterior shaping and the rim chamfer. Walnut oil for finishing; walnut oil is food-safe once set.
Method
Clamp the blank to the bench before starting the gouge work; the blank must not move under mallet blows. Use
stop cuts
at the bowl rim before hollowing begins, so the grain cannot tear past the rim boundary during the interior cuts.

Mark the bowl outline: a 25 cm circle on the top face, centred. Mark the target depth; 5 cm; on the side of the blank. Cut a stop cut around the full circle, 3 mm deep, with the sloyd knife. This sets the rim boundary.

Rough out the exterior: work the sloyd knife around the exterior of the blank to produce a round bowl profile. The base is flat; the sides curve inward toward the base from the rim. Work with the grain in push cuts, removing the corners in stages.

Hollow the interior with the No. 7 gouge, working from the rim inward: drive the gouge in 5 mm depth passes, always cutting from the rim toward the centre, never outward from the centre. Work in concentric rings, removing each ring before starting the next. Stop 5 mm above the target depth; the final 5 mm is for the finishing gouge.

Refine the bowl floor and walls with the No. 5 gouge in 12 mm width. Work in short arcs; 3 to 4 cm; removing the stepped surface left by the roughing pass. The bowl wall should be 8 mm at the rim and 10 mm at the floor. Check thickness regularly by pressing a finger inside and outside the bowl wall simultaneously.

Sand the interior through 80, 120, and 180 grit with the paper wrapped around a finger. Sand the exterior through 120 and 240 grit. Beech takes a fine surface at 240 grit; the grain pattern appears clearly after the first coat of oil.
Finishing

Apply walnut oil to all surfaces. Let it soak for 20 minutes and wipe off the excess. Walnut oil sets in 2 to 3 days at room temperature; the bowl is food-safe once the oil has set and the surface no longer feels tacky. Apply a second coat after the first has set. Re-oil monthly for the first year, then as needed when the surface looks dry.
Variations
Sycamore: paler and slightly softer than beech. Hollows more quickly; equally food-safe with walnut oil.
Walnut: darker, more visible grain. Treat for iron-tannin staining with a citric acid wipe before sanding. Food-safe with walnut oil.
Oval form: mark an oval instead of a circle (35 cm by 22 cm). The hollowing method is identical; the exterior shaping requires slightly more work at the narrow ends.
Care
Wash and dry promptly. Do not immerse or leave wet. Re-oil when the surface looks dry or after washing. Walnut oil re-application takes 20 minutes and extends the bowl's life indefinitely.

attrs.heading: What can go wrong
attrs.intro: Common problems with the carved beech fruit bowl.

items.symptom: Grain tears at the bowl rim during hollowing
items.cause: No stop cut before the gouge pass, or gouge cutting outward from the centre
items.fix: Always cut the stop cut at the rim before starting interior work. Drive the gouge from the rim inward, never outward; outward cuts lift the grain at the rim.

items.symptom: Bowl floor is uneven; high spots and ridges after gouge roughing
items.fix: Work in controlled rings; 3 to 4 mm depth per ring; and do not mix depth passes within one ring. The refinishing gouge will take out 3 to 4 mm of ridge if the roughing is not clean.

items.symptom: Bowl walls too thin; 4 mm or less; at one side
items.cause: Interior not centred on the blank before hollowing started
items.fix: Check the wall thickness every 2 rings from the start by feeling both surfaces simultaneously. If one side is getting thin, offset the subsequent rings slightly toward the thicker side.
